article_title: Former Bethesda artist says 'every single developer' has already brought up 95% of the complaints that made it to launch, but as Todd Howard told him, 'we can't do everything'
core_thesis: The article argues that most launch-day complaints about Bethesda games are already known to developers during production, but resource constraints and the need to ship force compromises. It highlights the gap between developer awareness and final product quality, using Todd Howard's quote 'We can do anything, but we can't do everything' to illustrate the trade-offs. The piece also contrasts Bethesda's post-launch support (e.g., Fallout 76's revival) with Howard's recent statements that Starfield will not receive a '2.0' overhaul, casting doubt on whether all games can be redeemed.
detailed_summary:
The article features an interview with retired Bethesda artist Dennis Mejillones, who worked on Skyrim, Fallout, Fallout 76, and Starfield. Mejillones states that approximately 95% of the criticisms players raise after launch were already voiced by developers during internal meetings. He emphasizes that developers are gamers themselves and see the same issues, but production realities—such as deadlines, budgets, and management decisions—prevent everything from being fixed. Todd Howard reportedly told the team, 'We can do anything, but we can't do everything,' acknowledging the necessity of prioritization.

The article uses Fallout 76 as an example of a game that improved significantly post-launch through continued updates, suggesting that Bethesda can turn things around when committed. However, it contrasts this with Howard's more recent comments that Starfield will not get a comprehensive '2.0' overhaul, implying that not all games will receive the same level of post-launch support. The author expresses skepticism about Starfield's future, noting that despite ongoing updates, the studio's leadership seems to have moved on. The piece ultimately reflects on the tension between developer awareness, production constraints, and the unpredictable nature of game development.

entities.name: Dennis Mejillones
entities.role: Former Bethesda artist who worked on Skyrim, Fallout, Fallout 76, and Starfield; provided the insider perspective on developer awareness of launch issues.

entities.name: Todd Howard
entities.role: Bethesda's executive producer, known for the quote 'We can do anything, but we can't do everything,' representing the management perspective on trade-offs.

entities.name: Bethesda Game Studios
entities.role: Game developer known for open-world RPGs; the article discusses their development process and post-launch support.

entities.name: Fallout 76
entities.role: Example of a game that improved significantly after launch through continued updates, used to illustrate potential for redemption.

entities.name: Starfield
entities.role: Recent Bethesda game that launched with mixed reception; the article notes Howard's statement that no '2.0' overhaul is planned.

key_data_points: Dennis Mejillones claims that 95% of post-launch complaints were already raised by developers during internal meetings, indicating that issues are known but not always resolved before shipping. (🟢 Relevant to your Red Teaming: This insight into development constraints can help you understand where vulnerabilities or design flaws may persist despite being known, informing your threat modeling.)

Todd Howard's quote 'We can do anything, but we can't do everything' encapsulates the trade-off between ambition and feasibility in game development. (🟢 Relevant to your programming and DevOps: This principle applies to any software project—prioritization is key; you can use this to justify focusing on critical features or security fixes.)

Fallout 76 was cited as a game that Bethesda continued to improve post-launch, eventually becoming 'basically good.' (🟢 Relevant to your homelabbing: This shows the value of iterative improvement and long-term support; you can apply similar patching and update strategies to your own projects.)

Todd Howard later stated that Starfield will not receive a '2.0' overhaul, suggesting that not all games get the same level of post-launch investment. (🟢 Relevant to your student perspective: This highlights the importance of first impressions and the risk of relying on future fixes; it's a lesson in project management and quality assurance.)
conclusions: Developers are often aware of most issues before launch, but resource constraints and deadlines prevent them from fixing everything. This conclusion is supported by Mejillones' firsthand account and Howard's quote, emphasizing that game development is a series of trade-offs.

Post-launch support can redeem a game, as seen with Fallout 76, but it is not guaranteed. The article contrasts Fallout 76's revival with Starfield's lack of a planned overhaul, suggesting that studio leadership and priorities play a decisive role.

The article underscores the gap between developer insight and final product quality, which is a common theme in software development. For users interested in cybersecurity or programming, this reinforces the need for thorough testing and realistic scope management.
personal_relevance_summary: This article offers a behind-the-scenes look at game development trade-offs that directly apply to your interests. As a programmer, you can relate to the challenge of balancing feature requests against time and resources—Howard's quote is a practical mantra for any project. For your Red Teaming focus, understanding that known issues often go unfixed due to prioritization helps you identify likely weak points in software. As a student, the article provides a realistic view of how even large teams struggle with quality, reinforcing the importance of iterative improvement and honest communication. The Fallout 76 example also demonstrates the value of persistent post-launch support, which you can emulate in your own homelab or DevOps projects.
extra_advice: Consider applying the 'we can't do everything' principle to your own projects: maintain a prioritized backlog of features and bugs, and be transparent with stakeholders (or yourself) about what will be deferred. For Red Teaming, use this insight to focus on areas that are likely deprioritized—such as edge cases or non-critical systems—as they may contain exploitable weaknesses.
